paper_title: VQQA: An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Despite rapid advancements in video generation models, aligning their outputs with complex user intent remains challenging. Existing test-time optimization methods are typically either computationally expensive or require white-box access to model internals. To address this, we present VQQA (Video Quality Question Answering), a unified, multi-agent framework generalizable across diverse input modalities and video generation tasks. By dynamically generating visual questions and using the resulting Vision-Language Model (VLM) critiques as semantic gradients, VQQA replaces traditional, passive evaluation metrics with human-interpretable, actionable feedback. This enables a highly efficient, closed-loop prompt optimization process via a black-box natural language interface. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VQQA effectively isolates and resolves visual artifacts, substantially improving generation quality in just a few refinement steps. Applicable to both text-to-video (T2V) and image-to-video (I2V) tasks, our method achieves absolute improvements of +11.57% on T2V-CompBench and +8.43% on VBench2 over vanilla generation, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art stochastic search and prompt optimization techniques.

Existing test-time optimization methods are typically either computationally expensive or require white-box access to model internals. To address this, we present VQQA (Video Quality Question Answering), unified, multi-agent framework generalizable across diverse input modalities and video generation tasks. By dynamically generating visual questions and using the resulting Vision-Language Model (VLM) critiques as semantic gradients, VQQA replaces traditional, passive evaluation metrics with human-interpretable, actionable feedback. This enables highly efficient, closed-loop prompt optimization process via black-box natural language interface. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VQQA effectively isolates and resolves visual artifacts, substantially improving generation quality in just few refinement steps. Applicable to both text-to-video (T2V) and image-to-video (I2V) tasks, our method achieves absolute improvements of +11.57% on T2V-CompBench and +8.43% on VBench2 over vanilla generation, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art stochastic search and prompt optimization techniques. Project Page: https://yiwen-song.github.io/vqqa/ 1. Introduction Driven by breakthroughs in diffusion and transformer architectures, visual generative models have revolutionized dynamic, high-resolution scene rendering (Blattmann et al., 2023; Google DeepMind, 2025; HaCohen et al., 2024; OpenAI, 2024; Peebles and Xie, 2023; Team et al., 2025; Wan et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2024). However, aligning these models with complex human intent remains challenging (Ma et al., 2025). Users frequently encounter compositional errors, temporal inconsistencies, and physical hallucinations (Liu et al., 2023b), which require tedious, trial-and-error prompt engineering. Furthermore, evaluation methods have not kept pace with model development. Early metrics (Salimans et al., 2016; Unterthiner et al., 2018) measure basic visual distributions but miss complex compositional alignment. Comprehensive benchmarks (Liao et al., 2024; Ling et al., 2025; Liu et al., 2023a; Meng et al., 2024) address this but require large, specialized model ensembles (Huang et al., 2024, 2025; Sun et al., 2025; Zheng et al., 2025), creating significant computational overhead. Alternatively, regressors or Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can predict human opinion scores (Ge et al., 2025; Kou et al., 2024; Lin et al., 2024). Yet, these systems act as passive observers, lacking the flexibility to adapt to new tasks or provide actionable feedback to correct generations. Concurrently, agentic workflows, where AI systems autonomously plan, execute, and refine their own output, show promise in overcoming traditional generation limits (Li et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2025). However, existing video test-time optimization typically relies on computationally intensive selection (e.g., VISTAs pairwise tournaments (Long et al., 2025)) or requires white-box access to model internals (Dalal et al., 2025). There is critical need for an interpretable, closed-loop system that diagnoses visual flaws and iteratively refines videos via black-box natural language interface. Corresponding author(s): yiwensong@google.com, yalesong@google.com VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement Figure 1 Videos generated by VQQA, showing improvements in categories such as numeracy, interaction, dynamic attributes, and spatial relationship. To address this, we propose VQQA (Video Quality Question Answering), unified multi-agent framework for holistic evaluation and iterative prompt refinement. By employing dynamic questionanswering paradigm instead of static rubrics, VQQA adapts seamlessly to diverse conditional tasks (e.g., T2V, I2V), transforming evaluation from passive metric into actionable feedback. VQQA operates via three specialized agents: Question Generation formulates targeted visual queries from inputs; Question Answering evaluates the video to isolate critical flaws; and Prompt Refinement uses these diagnostics as semantic gradient (Lee et al., 2025; Yuksekgonul et al., 2024) to optimize the prompt for the next iteration. To prevent semantic drift during refinement, VQQA employs Global Selection mechanism where VLM evaluates candidates against the initial prompt. Coupled with dynamic stopping criterion, this maximizes visual quality and minimizes compute overhead. Furthermore, VQQA is strictly model-agnostic, generalizing across models and modalities without task-specific fine-tuning. Our main contributions are: We propose VQQA, multi-agent framework that transforms video evaluation from passive benchmarking into dynamic question-answering paradigm, yielding actionable feedback across diverse generative tasks. We formalize test-time scaling for video generation as discrete, text-based optimization problem. By leveraging VLM-generated critiques as semantic gradients alongside Global Selection and dynamic stopping mechanism, we iteratively correct visual flaws without requiring model weight access, effectively preventing semantic drift and ensuring efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VQQA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art prompt optimization and sampling baselines across established benchmarks (T2V-CompBench (Sun et al., 2025), VBench2 (Zheng et al., 2025), VBench-I2V (Huang et al., 2025)) using both open-weights and proprietary models (Google DeepMind, 2025; Yang et al., 2024). 2 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement Figure 2 Qualitative example of VQQA iterative refinement process. Utilizing low-scoring QA pairs to isolate visual flaws, VQQA constructs an optimized prompt that successfully mitigates the localized artifacts in the next generation. 2. Related Work 2.1. Video Evaluation Frameworks The evaluation of generative video models has evolved from distribution-level metrics to semantic, agentic assessments. Early standard protocols relied on reference-based metrics like Fréchet Video Distance (FVD) (Unterthiner et al., 2018) and Inception Score (IS) (Salimans et al., 2016). While extended by Fréchet Video Motion Distance (FVMD) (Liu et al., 2024) to better capture temporal dynamics, these metrics correlate poorly with human perception at the instance level and fail to provide actionable feedback. To bridge this gap, the field shifted toward VLM-based evaluation. Methods like CLIPScore (Hessel et al., 2021) and BLIP-Score (Li et al., 2022) measure frame-text consistency but lack temporal awareness. More recent approaches leverage multimodal large language models as judges: VQAScore (Lin et al., 2024) computes probabilities of affirmative boolean answers, while T2VQA (Kou et al., 2024) and LMM-VQA (Ge et al., 2025) regress directly to Mean Opinion Scores (MOS). VideoScore2 (He et al., 2025b) advances this by employing think-before-you-score mechanism to generate Chainof-Thought (CoT) rationales. However, its reasoning remains constrained to pre-defined axes, limiting its flexibility in diagnosing unique, instance-specific artifacts. 3 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement Most recently, comprehensive benchmark suites and modular pipelines (Huang et al., 2024, 2025; Liu et al., 2023a; Sun et al., 2025; Zheng et al., 2025) have decomposed quality into disentangled axes. While frameworks like Evaluation Agent (Zhang et al., 2025) employ human-like, multi-round evaluation, they remain primarily designed for passive performance benchmarking. In contrast, VQQA dynamically generates context-dependent questions that serve as direct language interface for downstream refinement. 2.2. Prompt Optimization for Video Generation Prompt engineering has become essential for unlocking the capabilities of frozen generative models. Early text-based approaches like APE (Zhou et al., 2022) and Promptist (Hao et al., 2023) utilized iterative search to align prompts with model preferences. In the visual domain, methods like PromptA-Video (Ji et al., 2025) and VPO (Cheng et al., 2025) adapt these techniques to text-to-video diffusion. VPO, for instance, optimizes prompts for harmlessness, accuracy, and helpfulness using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) (Rafailov et al., 2023). However, most of these methods operate in an open-loop fashionoptimizing prompts based on dataset-level priors rather than the specific visual artifacts of the current generation. While self-correcting approaches are widely established in LLMs (e.g., Self-Refine (Madaan et al., 2023), Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023)), systems for explicit self-critique and revision remain relatively underexplored in video generation. Recent exceptions include VideoAgent (Soni et al., 2024) for robotic planning and VideoRepair (Lee et al., 2024), which employs detect-and-patch strategy to fix localized misalignments. While effective for isolated errors, localized methods cannot address global inconsistencies like temporal flow. VQQA overcomes this via holistic closed-loop system, iteratively updating the entire prompt based on granular visual evidence to correct both local and global failures. 2.3. Test-Time Scaling for Video Generation Scaling compute at inference time has proven effective for complex tasks. In LLMs, techniques like Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) (Yao et al., 2023) and Chain-of-Verification (Dhuliawala et al., 2024) demonstrate that iterative reasoning boosts performance without retraining. In video generation, inference-time scaling typically manifests as rejection sampling or trajectory search. Beyond the standard Best-of-N approach, Video-T1 (Liu et al., 2025) formalizes test-time scaling as trajectory search problem using verifiers, while VISTA (Long et al., 2025) implements an agentic self-improving loop. Other approaches intervene directly in generative physics: VideoTTT (Dalal et al., 2025) applies gradient updates to RNN-based hidden states, while EvoSearch (He et al., 2025a) mutates initial noise and intermediate latents to discover higher-quality trajectories. However, existing approaches face distinct limitations. Gradient-based methods like Video-TTT and EvoSearch require white-box access to model internals, rendering them incompatible with commercial APIs. Conversely, agentic frameworks like VISTA incur massive computational costs by requiring large candidate pools to identify improvements. VQQA proposes an alternative inspired by text-based optimization. Building on TextGrad (Yuksekgonul et al., 2024) and Feedback Descent (Lee et al., 2025), which formalize backpropagation-like feedback via natural language, VQQA treats the prompt as the optimization variable. By using VLM-guided feedback as semantic gradients, VQQA achieves precise adaptation and error correction strictly through natural language interface, bypassing the need for weight access or exhaustive sampling. 4 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement 3. Methodology 3.1. Problem Formulation 3.1.1. Video Evaluation System Let 𝑝 denote text prompt, 𝐶 = {𝑐1, 𝑐2, . . . , 𝑐𝑛} set of generation conditions (e.g., reference images), and 𝑀 pre-trained video generation model. The sampled video 𝑣 is: 𝑣 = 𝑀 ( 𝑝, 𝐶) (1) The objective of an evaluation system is to design reward function 𝑓 that produces both quantitative quality score 𝑆 𝑓 and qualitative linguistic rationale 𝑅 𝑓 based on the provided inputs: (𝑆 𝑓 , 𝑅 𝑓 ) = 𝑓 (𝑣, 𝑝, 𝐶) (2) Given an external ground-truth evaluation system 𝑔 (e.g., human Mean Opinion Scores or established benchmark auto-raters), 𝑓 minimizes the discrepancy between its predicted score 𝑆 𝑓 and the target score 𝑆𝑔 = 𝑔(𝑣, 𝑝, 𝐶): 𝑓 = argmin 𝔼 (cid:2)Δ(𝑆𝑔, 𝑆 𝑓 )(cid:3) 𝑓 (3) Crucially, 𝑓 requires interpretability; it must approximate the scalar score and generate humaninterpretable reasoning 𝑅 𝑓 to justify evaluations and facilitate downstream refinement (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017). 3.1.2. Iterative Refinement via Test-Time Training (TTT) Function 𝑓 ultimately guides the optimization of prompt 𝑝. We formulate this as Test-Time Training (TTT) task to find an optimal prompt 𝑝 from the prompt space P: 𝑝 = argmax 𝑝 𝑔(𝑀 ( 𝑝, 𝐶), 𝑝, 𝐶) (4) Since 𝑔 is typically unknown and non-differentiable at inference time, we use 𝑓 𝑓 as proxy. This transforms video refinement into discrete prompt-space optimization process (Lee et al., 2025; Yuksekgonul et al., 2024). Rather than traditional gradient descent, which is ill-defined for discrete text, we use the reasoning component 𝑅 𝑓 ,𝑡 at step 𝑡 as semantic gradient to identify visual flaws. Letting (𝑆 𝑓 ,𝑡, 𝑅 𝑓 ,𝑡) = 𝑓 (𝑣𝑡, 𝑝𝑡, 𝐶), we define the iterative update rule as: where the VLM acts as refinement operator, leveraging the critique 𝑅 𝑓 ,𝑡 to rectify flaws in the subsequent generation. 𝑝𝑡+1 = VLM( 𝑝𝑡, 𝑅 𝑓 ,𝑡) (5) 3.2. The VQQA Framework 3.2.1. Multi-Agent Architecture As shown in Figure 3, VQQA decomposes interpretable video evaluation and iterative refinement into three specialized agents: 5 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement Figure 3 The VQQA framework: Given generation conditions 𝐶 and prompt 𝑝𝑡, the model 𝑀 produces video 𝑣𝑡. The multi-agent framework uses Question Generation (QG) agent to formulate visual queries 𝑄 and Question Answering (QA) agent to evaluate the video and produce score report. These outputs inform the Prompt Refinement (PR) agent, which uses semantic gradient to update the prompt for the next iteration. Finally, Global VLM Rater assesses the candidate set of generated videos against the original conditions to select the optimal video 𝑣. Question Generation (QG) Agent: Analyzes the video 𝑣, prompt 𝑝, and conditions 𝐶 to dynamically generate set of questions 𝑄 across three dimensions: Video-Prompt Alignment, Visual Quality, and Condition Fidelity (when additional conditions 𝐶 are provided). This categorization directly mirrors the primary evaluation axes established by comprehensive benchmarks, ensuring robust and standardized coverage of critical failure modes. Question Answering (QA) Agent: Acts as the primary evaluator, inspecting video 𝑣 against questions 𝑄 to assign normalized scores 𝑠 [0, 100] per question, constructing detailed diagnostic map of critical visual flaws identified from the video. Prompt Refinement (PR) Agent: Synthesizes QA feedback into an optimized prompt 𝑝𝑡+1. By processing multiple low-scoring QA pairs (the semantic gradient), the agent formulates revised prompt that concurrently mitigates these localized errors in the next iteration. Complete implementation details, including the prompts, models and parameters, are provided in Appendix C. 3.2.2. Global Selection and Convergence The iterative refinement process is inherently stochastic, as it samples different trajectories within the latent space of the generator 𝑀. To ensure the framework identifies the optimal generation and terminates efficiently, we define both global selection mechanism and convergence criterion. 6 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement Global Selection To prevent semantic driftwhere localized refinements cause deviation from overarching user intentwe employ Global VLM Rater to perform holistic, post-hoc evaluation of the candidate set = {𝑣1, 𝑣2, . . . , 𝑣𝑁 }. The rater assesses each candidate against the original prompt 𝑝0: On scale of 0-100, how well does this video match original prompt (and reference images)? Respond with only number between 0 and 100. The rater assigns Global Score (𝐺𝑆) to each video, and the final output 𝑣 is selected as the candidate that maximizes alignment: 𝑣 = 𝑣𝑖, where 𝑖 = argmax 𝑖 {1,...,𝑁 } 𝐺𝑆(𝑣𝑖, 𝑝0, 𝐶) (6) This mechanism ensures that while the Prompt Refinement Agent explores variations to enhance visual quality, the final selection remains anchored to the users primary goals. Unlike VQAScore (Lin et al., 2024), which relies on models fine-tuned specifically for text-visual alignment, our approach leverages the inherent visual reasoning capabilities of VLMs. This flexibility allows 𝐺𝑆 to evaluate fidelity across multiple generation conditions without task-specific training. Convergence Criterion To trade-off between inference cost and quality, we employ early stopping 𝑡 = max𝜏 {1,...,𝑡} 𝐺𝑆(𝑣𝜏). Refinement terminates at step based on the running maximum Global Score 𝑆 𝑡 if either condition is met: 1. Target Satisfaction: The system generates video that meets the ideal quality standard, denoted by threshold 𝛾 (e.g., 𝛾 = 100). (7) 2. Performance Saturation: The global maximum score stagnates over patience window 𝑘, 𝑆 𝑡 𝛾 where improvement falls below marginal 𝜖: Δ(𝑆 𝑡 , 𝑆 𝑡𝑘) 𝜖 (8) This ensures that the framework halts immediately upon achieving an optimal result or when additional compute yields no additional improvement. 4. Experiments 4.1. Experimental Setup 4.1.1. Tasks We evaluate the VQQA framework on two video generation tasks: Text-to-Video (T2V): Given text prompt 𝑝, the objective is to generate video 𝑣 = 𝑀 ( 𝑝) that accurately reflects the semantic and temporal content of 𝑝. Image-to-Video (I2V): Given prompt 𝑝 and reference images 𝐶 = {𝑖1, . . . , 𝑖𝑚}, the goal is to synthesize video 𝑣 = 𝑀 ( 𝑝, 𝐶) adhering to the textual prompt while maintaining high visual fidelity to the reference image(s). 7 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement Table 1 T2V-CompBench evaluation results on CogVideoX-5B. Unlike traditional pipelines hard-coded for specific modalities, VQQA acts as task-agnostic opIts agents dynamically adapt visual queries to the provided condition set (text-only or timizer. text+images). This versatility allows us to seamlessly apply the exact same iterative refinement process to both paradigms without architectural modifications. 4.1.2. Baselines We compare our method against established optimization frameworks and stochastic search strategies using various scoring functions: Video Prompt Optimization (VPO) (Cheng et al., 2025): primary baseline for prompt refinement. VPO is two-stage framework optimizing for harmlessness, accuracy, and helpfulness. It refines prompts to enhance both generation quality and safety. Best-of-N (BoN) (Stiennon et al., 2020): stochastic search baseline sampling 𝑁 candidates {𝑣1, . . . , 𝑣𝑁 } from the initial prompt 𝑝0, selecting the optimal video via reward function. We VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement implement BoN with three distinct scoring mechanisms: BoN with VQAScore (Lin et al., 2024): VQAScore is an automated metric based on finetuned Visual Question Answering (VQA) models. It measures alignment by averaging confidence scores of boolean questions (e.g., Does this video show [text]?) across video frames. BoN with VideoScore2 (He et al., 2025b): VideoScore2 is regression-based reward model trained on large-scale human preference data. Unlike frame-averaging metrics, it uses spatio-temporal representation to directly predict human-like quality and alignment scores for an entire video sequence. BoN with VLM-Rating: Prompts multimodal VLMs to act as judges, rating video-condition alignment on 0100 scale. 4.1.3. Benchmarks We evaluate our method on the following benchmarks: T2V-CompBench (Sun et al., 2025): text-to-video benchmark comprising 1,400 prompts across seven categories. It is designed to evaluate compositional generation, measuring how effectively models integrate multiple objects, actions, and attributes into coherent, temporally consistent videos. VBench2 (Huang et al., 2024): text-to-video evaluation suite focused on measuring intrinsic faithfulness. VBench2 uses multi-agent VLM system to evaluate five high-level dimensions: Human Fidelity, Controllability, Creativity, Physics, and Commonsense. This framework enables systematic assessment of models ability to reduce complex physical and anatomical hallucinations. VBench-I2V (Huang et al., 2025): An extension of VBench for image-to-video generation. It evaluates how faithfully generated video aligns with reference image, assessing temporal consistency, motion magnitude, and the preservation of the images identity and semantic details. 4.2. Results 4.2.1. T2V-CompBench Table 1 shows our VQQA framework consistently outperforming both the vanilla generation and all baselines on T2V-CompBench. Using Gemini-3-Pro, VQQA achieves the highest average score (53.46%), delivering an absolute improvement of +11.57% over vanilla generation and +4.76% over the strongest baseline (VQAScore). VQQA with GPT-4o secures the second-best average (51.30%), showing the superiority of our approach regardless of the underlying VLM. The iterative refinement process effectively resolves compositional errors, yielding substantial absolute gains across all categories, especially in consistent-attribute (+22.94%), spatial understanding (+14.31%), and numeracy (+13.85%). 4.2.2. VBench2 Results on the VBench2 benchmark  (Table 2)  further validate our approach. The Gemini-3-Pro and GPT-4o variants of VQQA achieve the highest (50.41%) and second-highest (48.18%) total scores, respectively. VQQA with Gemini-3-Pro provides +8.43% absolute increase over the vanilla baseline and surpasses the best competing method (VQAScore) by +3.46%. 9 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement Table 3 VBench-I2V evaluation results on CogVideoX-5B-I2V. VBench-I2V Evaluation results on the VBench-I2V benchmark  (Table 3)  further demonstrate the versatility of the VQQA framework on the I2V task. Despite the high saturation of this benchmark, VQQA with Gemini-3-Pro achieves the highest performance across all evaluated axes, improving upon vanilla generation by +1.24% and the strongest Best-of-N baseline by +0.23%. Furthermore, VQQA exhibits remarkable efficiency, requiring an average of only 1.6 iterations to satisfy the tasks stopping criterion. 4.3. Analysis 4.3.1. Quality of Generated Questions To evaluate the coverage and effectiveness of VQQAs generated questions, we use the VideoFeedback2 (He et al., 2025b) test split. We construct ground-truth (GT) set of visual flaws by prompting GPT-5.2 (Singh et al., 2025) to extract discrete problems from the datasets reasoning trajectories. judge model maps each GT problem to the specific VQQA questions designed to detect it. We evaluate recall at two levels: (1) Question Recall (Q-Recall), the percentage of GT problems covered by at least one generated question; and (2) End-to-End (E2E) Recall, the percentage of GT problems where the corresponding VQQA question correctly receives sub-threshold score from the QA agent. We measure precision based on the generated questions relevance to the input video and prompt, assessed via binary classification by judge model. We prioritize relevance over strict GT mapping for two reasons: (i) the GT set, derived from Claude-4-Sonnet (Anthropic, 2025) trajectories, may be incomplete; and (ii) VQQA proactively probes for potential visual artifacts. Consequently, contextually relevant question remains valuable even if no flaw is ultimately confirmed. Finally, we compare VQQA against zero-shot VLM baseline that directly identifies visual flaws. As Table 4 demonstrates, while both methods maintain near-perfect precision (>99%), VQQA yields 10 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement (a) VQQA performance across iterations (b) Convergence parameter sensitivity Figure 4 Convergence analysis of VQQA on T2V-CompBench. Evaluations are performed for CogVideoX-5B generations using Gemini-3-Pro. (a) Correlations between the maximum global score 𝑆 𝑡 and the T2V-CompBench metric across optimization steps. The blue shaded region indicates 1 standard deviation of 𝑆 𝑡 across the 1400 evaluated samples. The detailed performance breakdown for each individual category is provided in Appendix C.4. (b) Sensitivity analysis of average iterations to converge given the patience window 𝑘 and saturation threshold 𝜖 (Equation (8)). significant 11.9% improvement in E2E-Recall over the baseline. This substantial gain in recall means our system captures considerably wider array of visual artifacts, delivering the robust feedback necessary to effectively correct errors and improve subsequent video generations. Qualitative analysis indicates that the marginal precision deficits in both methods primarily stem from infrequent VLM hallucinations during evaluation. 4.3.2. Convergence and Efficiency To evaluate the efficiency of the proposed stopping criterion (Equation (8)), we analyze optimization trajectories over horizon of 𝑁 = 10 steps. As illustrated in Figure 4, both the agents internal score 𝑆 𝑡 and the external T2V-CompBench metrics exhibit rapid gains in the initial few iterations, reaching saturation plateau shortly thereafter. The sensitivity analysis in Figure 4b demonstrates that for standard patience window 𝑘 = 3, the algorithm converges within 3.80 to 4.22 iterations across various thresholds. This aligns with the visual evidence in Figure 4a, where the red dashed line (Iter=4) indicates the majority of performance gains are captured within the first four rounds. These results confirm that our dynamic termination mechanism effectively prevents redundant inference steps by halting once the semantic gradient vanishes, thereby maximizing computational efficiency without sacrificing the video quality gain accumulated through iterations. 4.4. Ablation Studies 4.4.1. Generalizability to Proprietary Models To assess the model-agnostic nature of VQQA, we applied our prompt optimization pipeline to the proprietary Google Veo 3.1 model (Google DeepMind, 2025). As shown in Table 5, VQQA remains 11 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement Table 5 T2V-CompBench evaluation results on Veo3.1. After four rounds, VQQA improved the overall T2V-CompBench average score by an absolute margin of +5.88% (from 55.93% to 61.81%), outperforming the Best-of-N approaches using VQAScore (+3.58%) and Gemini-3-Pro (+3.08%). Furthermore, VQQA achieves the highest performance across the majority of compositional dimensions. 4.4.2. Global Selection Mechanism We investigate the contribution of the Global Selection (Equation (6)) by comparing the selection function against two variants: 1. Last-Iter Selection (𝑣𝑁): We disable the Global Rater and simply accept the output of the final iteration. performance drop here indicates semantic drift, where the Prompt Refinement Agent optimizes for localized visual details at the expense of original user intent. As shown in Figure 5, omitting global selection decreases the overall average score by 1.02%, confirming that unanchored refinement drifts from primary constraints. 2. Average-QA Selection: We replace the Global Score 𝐺𝑆 with the average score derived from the QA Agents internal questions. This tests whether holistic, post-hoc evaluation supersedes the aggregated granular scores used during optimization. This variant underperforms the proposed global selection by an average margin of 1.86%. This confirms that while granular QA scores provide effective semantic gradients for localized refinement, their unweighted aggregation 12 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement Table 6 Ablation Study on GlobalScore in the loop. Overall, Figure 5 demonstrates the necessity of decoupling the iterative feedback loop from the final selection criterion. Because prompt refinement targets specific visual bottlenecks, it does not guarantee monotonic improvement in holistic text-to-video alignment at every step. By employing the Global Score to evaluate the candidate pool against the original prompt, the proposed mechanism successfully mitigates semantic drift. This macro-level perspective ensures the final output achieves the optimal balance across critical dimensions, outperforming alternative selection strategies. 4.4.3. Prompt Refinement: GS-in-the-loop (or not?) To evaluate if the global score 𝐺𝑆 can further impact the optimization process, we tried one variation of our method, which is injecting the global score into the Prompt Refinement (PR) agents context history, analyzing whether exposing the PR agent to this high-level metric aids convergence or introduces noise that hampers specific visual corrections. As Table 6 shows, incorporating 𝐺𝑆 during iterative refinement degrades average generation quality (53.11% vs. the standard 53.77%). Specifically, excluding 𝐺𝑆 from the PR context better resolves fine-grained compositional errors, outperforming the GS-in-the-loop variant in attribute consistency (84.01% vs. 82.85%), spatial understanding (66.01% vs. 65.76%), and numeracy (52.29% vs. 48.68%). This indicates that providing holistic metric during refinement introduces optimization noise. When exposed to 𝐺𝑆, the agent tends to become distracted from the localized, specific bottlenecks identified by the granular QA pairs. It attempts to prematurely optimize the global score rather than sequentially resolving isolated visual errors. Therefore, strictly decoupling the granular feedback loop (used for targeted refinement) from the global score (used strictly for final candidate selection) yields the most robust compositional improvements. 5. Conclusion In this paper, we introduced VQQA, novel multi-agent framework that transforms the passive evaluation of video generation models into an active, closed-loop refinement process. By dynamically generating visual questions and leveraging Vision-Language Model critiques as semantic gradients, VQQA enables precise, iterative prompt optimization through black-box natural language interface. Extensive evaluations across both T2V and I2V tasks demonstrate that VQQA yields significant 13 VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement improvements over strong baselines requiring very few iterations, generalizing seamlessly across open-weights and proprietary models. Ultimately, VQQA provides scalable, task-agnostic solution for aligning visual generative models with complex human intent, paving the way for more controllable and interpretable AI-driven content creation.

VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement A. Limitations While VQQA demonstrates strong performance, its efficacy is inherently bounded by its underlying foundation models. As black-box optimizer, it relies on Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for reasoning and base video models for generation. Consequently, it cannot rectify fundamental architectural flaws, synthesize out-of-distribution concepts, or entirely eliminate noisy semantic gradients from VLM hallucinations. However, its model-agnostic design ensures these constraints will naturally diminish as foundation models advance. Furthermore, the iterative multi-agent loop requires sequential generation and querying, resulting in higher inference latency than parallelizable methods like Bestof-𝑁. Nevertheless, VQQA justifies this sequential compute cost by achieving rapid convergence and superior alignment with complex user intent. B. Inference Cost We analyze the number of VLM calls required by our pipeline compared to the baseline methods. Let 𝑁 denote the number of sampled candidates in the Best-of-𝑁 strategy, and 𝑇 denote the number of optimization iterations in VQQA. As detailed in Table 7, standard Best-of-𝑁 approaches require 𝑁 parallel VLM calls to evaluate the pre-generated candidate pool. In contrast, VQQA actively forms semantic gradient at each step, requiring multiple VLM calls per iteration: Question Generation (2 calls for T2V, plus 𝑘 calls for 𝑘 reference images in I2V settings), Question Answering (1 call), and Prompt Refinement (1 call). This results in (4 + 𝑘)𝑇 generation-phase calls. The Global Rater then evaluates the 𝑇 + 1 generated candidates (the initial generation plus the 𝑇 optimized outputs), adding 𝑇 + 1 evaluation calls. While the theoretical maximum number of VLM calls for VQQA is (5 + 𝑘)𝑇 + 1, our method converges rapidly in practice due to the dynamic stopping criterion. On the T2V-CompBench dataset (𝑘 = 0), VQQA converges after an average of 𝑇 = 1.245 optimization rounds using the Gemini-3Pro (Pichai et al., 2025) model. By substituting this average into our cost formulation, the expected total number of VLM calls for VQQA is approximately 7.23. This demonstrates that VQQA achieves its significant improvements in visual quality and prompt alignment with an inference footprint comparable to standard Best-of-5 (𝑁 = 5) baseline. Experiment Details C.1. Models C.1.1. Vision-Language Models (VLMs) Gemini API: We accessed the Gemini model (gemini-3-pro-preview and gemini-3-flash -preview) (Pichai et al., 2025) via the Google Cloud Vertex AI platform. OpenAI API: We utilized OpenAI models (gpt-4o-2024-08-06 (Hurst et al., 2024), gpt5.1-2025-11-13 and gpt-5.2-2025-12-11 (Singh et al., 2025)) accessed via the official OpenAI API. C.1.2. Video Generation Models CogVideoX-5B: (Yang et al., 2024) Accessed via the Hugging Face diffusers library (THUDM/ CogVideoX-5b). Generation parameters were set to 50 inference steps, 41 frames, and guidance scale of 6.0. Veo 3.1: (Google DeepMind, 2025) Accessed via the Google Cloud Vertex AI API (veo-3.1-gene rate-preview). Videos were generated with 16:9 aspect ratio using default parameters. Generations that triggered safety filters were replaced with blank videos to maintain batch consistency. CogVideoX-5B-I2V: (Yang et al., 2024) Accessed via the diffusers library (THUDM/CogVi deoX-5b-I2V). Generation parameters were set to 50 inference steps, 49 frames, and guidance scale of 6.0. C.1.3. Video Scoring Models VPO: Accessed via the Hugging Face CLI (CCCCCC/VPO-5B) (Cheng et al., 2025). This model is specifically fine-tuned to optimize user prompts for CogVideoX-5B. VQAScore: (Lin et al., 2024) Accessed via the t2v_metrics library. We employed llavaonevision-qwen2-7b-ov as the backbone model to evaluate video-text alignment. VideoScore2: (He et al., 2025b) Accessed via the Hugging Face API (TIGER-Lab/VideoSco -re2). The models text-to-video alignment score was utilized as the reward signal during Best-of-N (BoN) selection. C.2. Video Generation Seeds To ensure fair comparison between the BoN and VQQA methods, the initial video generation seed is fixed at 17 across all experiments for models that accept seed inputs (i.e., CogVideoX-5B and CogVideoX5B-I2V; note that Veo 3.1 (Google DeepMind, 2025) does not accept seed input). For the second and all subsequent generations during both BoN and VQQA prompt optimization, the seed 𝑠 is sampled uniformly at random: 𝑠 (0, 232 1) (9) In VBench2 (Zheng et al., 2025) and VBench-I2V (Huang et al., 2025), where each prompt correlates to multiple sampled videos, optimization is performed per sample rather than per prompt. Assuming 𝑛 samples are required per prompt, the seed used for sample index 𝑘 in the initial iteration is deterministically set to 17 + 100𝑘. All subsequent iterations utilize random sampling as specified in Equation (9). VQQA : An Agentic Approach for Video Evaluation and Quality Improvement Figure 6 Full convergence analysis of VQQA on T2V-CompBench. Evaluations are performed using CogVideoX-5B as the generator and Gemini-3-Pro as the VLM. For the Prompt Optimizer (VPO) baseline, which requires only single video generation, random seed is utilized. C.3. Parameters Across all tasksincluding question generation, question answering, video analysis and prompt optimizationwe strictly set the generation temperature to 0.0. This ensures deterministic outputs, maximizes response stability, and guarantees the reproducibility of our experiments. C.4. Long-horizon runs Figure 6 details the performance breakdown for each T2V-CompBench category over 10 VQQA prompt optimization iterations. It illustrates the correlation between the running maximum global score 𝑆 𝑡 and the benchmark metric across optimization steps. Notably, most semantic performance gains occur within the first four rounds across nearly all categories, highlighting our methods efficiency.
